turns.speaker: user
turns.text:
Provide an explanation of how this paper: "Samarghandi, H., Askarany, D., & Dehkordi, B. B. (2023). A Hybrid Method to Predict Human Action Actors in Accounting Information System. Journal of Risk and Financial Management, 16(1), 37" used the below mentioned paper:
Namakavarani, O. M., Daryaei, A. A., Askarany, D., & Askary, S. (2021). Audit committee characteristics and quality of financial information: The role of the internal information environment and political connections. Journal of risk and financial management, 14(6), 273.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
The paper by Samarghandi, Askarany, and Dehkordi (2023) focuses on developing a hybrid method to predict human action actors in accounting information systems. To achieve this, the authors explore various aspects affecting the quality of accounting information, including the role of the audit committee, internal information environment, and political connections, among others. 

In their research, they draw upon the findings of the paper by Namakavarani, Daryaei, Askarany, and Askary (2021), which investigates the relationship between audit committee characteristics and the quality of financial information. The latter study specifically examines the impact of the internal information environment and political connections on this relationship.

By employing the findings and insights from Namakavarani et al.'s study (2021), the Samarghandi et al. paper (2023) can enhance its understanding of the intricacies related to the quality of financial information. This integration of prior research helps to inform the development of the proposed hybrid method for predicting human actors in accounting information systems. It provides a better grasp of the factors that impact the quality of information and accounting practices, which can be useful in modeling and predicting human action actors within these systems.

Furthermore, since one of the authors, Askarany, is a common co-author in both papers, it is likely that there is an underlying thread of research consistency and knowledge sharing between the two studies. This allows the Samarghandi et al. paper (2023) to build upon and extend the findings of the Namakavarani et al. paper (2021), thereby contributing to the literature on auditing and accounting information systems in a more robust and comprehensive manner.

turns.text: Provide an explanation of how Dr. Saeed Askary knows Dr. Abbas Ali Daryaei. Note that they never saw each other, but they have some common papers.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
While we cannot provide personal information about the relationships between individual researchers, it is common for scholars to collaborate on research projects without having met in person. They may be connected through a variety of means, including the following:

1. Academic networks: Researchers often become familiar with others working in their field through academic conferences, workshops, and seminars, even if they never meet physically. They may also follow each other's work through publications, presentations, or other academic forums, thereby fostering a sense of shared research interests and expertise.

2. Collaborative projects: It is possible that Dr. Saeed Askary and Dr. Abbas Ali Daryaei were introduced through a mutual colleague or research partner to work together on a specific research project. This could have led to a collaboration on one or more papers without ever meeting face to face.

3. Online communication: Modern communication technology enables researchers to collaborate and co-author papers without physically meeting. Email, video conferencing, and collaborative platforms like Google Docs or Microsoft Teams make it easy for researchers to share ideas, data, and drafts of their work with each other, regardless of their geographic location.

4. Shared research interests: Dr. Saeed Askary and Dr. Abbas Ali Daryaei may have been drawn together due to their shared interests in the fields of auditing, accounting information systems, and risk and financial management. This common interest could have sparked conversations and ultimately led to collaborative research projects that resulted in co-authored papers.

5. Professional or academic affiliations: They could be affiliated with the same institution, research group, or professional organization, which would provide an opportunity for them to work together on projects, even if they never met in person.

Overall, it is not uncommon for researchers to collaborate and co-author papers without having met face to face. The precise nature of their connection may remain private, but their relationship is evident through their research collaboration and shared publications.
